title: The Rage of Underpaid Caregivers
dek: *The fury you feel when you're wiping someone else's body for minimum wage isn't a character flaw. It's your nervous system telling the truth about labor that society calls sacred but refuses to pay for. Here's how to honor that anger without letting it consume you, and why pretending it isn't there makes everything worse.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Caregivers carry anger not because they lack compassion but because their bodies register the gap between what they give and what they receive. This rage is an intelligent signal pointing to systemic exploitation disguised as virtue. Learning to listen to it without shame transforms burnout into boundary-setting power.

Most people think anger exists to make us destructive. But what if the opposite is true? What if anger is the part of you that still believes you deserve better, the last ember of self-respect that hasn't been smothered by the language of sacrifice and calling? When you're earning $13 an hour to feed, bathe, and comfort someone who cannot do those things for themselves, and your own bills are late, and your body aches in three places you can't afford to get checked, anger isn't the problem. Anger is the messenger telling you that something about this arrangement is wrong at the root. In my own practice, I've sat with enough caregivers to know that the anger arrives in waves, often at the strangest moments. Not during the hardest tasks, but afterward. In the car. In the shower. In the sixty seconds before sleep when your mind finally stops moving and the feeling rises up like water you've been holding back all day. ## The Economic Violence Hiding Behind Virtue Language We talk about caregiving as though it's a calling, a ministry, a labor of love. And maybe it is. But love doesn't pay rent. Calling doesn't cover the co-pay when your back gives out from lifting a 180-pound body five times in a shift. The same society that applauds caregivers as heroes pays them less than the people who park cars or pour coffee, and then wonders why turnover is brutal and compassion fatigue is epidemic. Soraya Chemaly writes about how anger in women, particularly women in service roles, is pathologized as hysteria or bitterness when it's actually a rational response to being systematically undervalued. Caregivers, who are disproportionately women and people of color, are expected to perform emotional labor on top of physical labor, to smile through indignity, to absorb verbal abuse from patients and families as though it's part of the job description. When you get angry about that, you're not failing at compassion. You're succeeding at reality-testing. The economic structure is simple and ugly. Care work is undercompensated because it's associated with tasks women have historically done for free in the home. The market has decided that intimacy and tenderness have no monetary value, which means the people who provide them professionally are treated as expendable. You can see this in the wages, in the benefits, in the fact that many caregivers qualify for the same public assistance programs their clients use. ## What Your Body Knows Before Your Mind Catches Up Anger lives in the body first. It's a tightness in the jaw, a heat behind the eyes, a clenching in the fists you don't notice until you open your hands and see the half-moon marks your nails left in your palms. When you're underpaid and overworked, your nervous system doesn't need a spreadsheet to know you're being exploited. It feels the imbalance as a threat. The CDC's guidance on stress and the body confirms what caregivers already know from lived experience: chronic stress rewires your physiology. Cortisol stays elevated. Blood pressure climbs. Sleep fragments. The anger you're carrying isn't just emotional, it's chemical, a full-system alarm that the conditions you're working under are unsustainable. Karla McLaren talks about anger as a boundary-setting emotion, the one that helps you identify where your limits have been crossed and what needs to change. In caregiving, those boundaries get violated constantly. You're asked to stay late without notice. You're assigned more clients than you can safely attend to. You're told that if you really cared, you'd figure it out, as though caring harder could split you into two people or add hours to the day. Your body registers all of this. The anger is your system's way of saying: this is too much, this is not fair, something has to give. Ignoring that signal doesn't make you more professional. It makes you more likely to break down in ways that take months to recover from. ## The Myth of the Patient Saint There's a story we tell about good caregivers, and it goes like this: they are endlessly patient, never resentful, fueled by some boundless well of compassion that never runs dry. This story is a lie, and it's a lie that serves the people who profit from your labor, not the people who perform it. Real caregivers get frustrated. They get bone-tired. They have moments where they resent the person they're caring for, not because that person has done anything wrong, but because the situation itself is impossible. You can love your work and still hate the conditions. You can feel genuine affection for a client and still want to scream when their family criticizes you for something outside your control while paying you less than they spend on lawn care. Thich Nhat Hanh wrote about anger as a crying baby that needs to be held, not rejected. The practice isn't to pretend you're not angry, it's to turn toward the anger with curiosity and tenderness. What is it trying to protect? What need is going unmet? When you're caring for others all day, the idea of caring for your own anger might feel like one more impossible task. But it's actually the key to not drowning. In our experience, the caregivers who last the longest are not the ones who suppress their anger most successfully. They're the ones who've learned to let it inform their choices. They're the ones who can say, "I felt rage when that happened, and that tells me I need to advocate for a schedule change" or "This anger is showing me I can't keep working for an agency that treats me like this." ## A Five-Minute Practice for When the Anger Arrives You don't need a meditation cushion or an hour of silence. You need something you can do in a car between shifts, in a bathroom stall, in the narrow margin of your own life. Sit down if you can. If you can't, stand. Close your eyes or soften your gaze. Put one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Breathe in for a count of four. Hold for four. Breathe out for six. Repeat three times. Now say this out loud or in your mind: "I am angry because I am not being treated fairly. This anger is trying to protect me. I don't have to act on it right now, but I don't have to push it away either." Notice where the anger sits in your body. Is it in your throat? Your shoulders? Your gut? Just notice. Don't try to fix it. Breathe into that spot for three more breaths. When you're ready, open your eyes. You haven't solved anything, but you've done something crucial. You've acknowledged the anger instead of swallowing it. That acknowledgment is the beginning of everything that comes next. ## The Difference Between Rage and Boundaries Rage is what happens when anger gets ignored for so long it ferments. It's the explosion after a thousand small violations. Boundaries are what you build when you listen to anger early, when it's still a whisper and not a scream. Setting a boundary as a caregiver feels impossible because you've been trained to see it as selfish. But a boundary isn't about withholding care. It's about defining the terms under which you can provide care without destroying yourself. It sounds like: "I can't take an extra shift this week." It sounds like: "I need my breaks, and I need them to be uninterrupted." It sounds like: "If you speak to me that way again, I will leave the room." Some employers will respect those boundaries. Many won't. That's diagnostic. It tells you whether you're working in a place that sees you as human or as a resource to be extracted. Across the articles we've published on this site, one pattern shows up again and again: anger becomes useful when it gives you the clarity to leave what's harming you, even when leaving is hard. Adaptogens like [Organic India Ashwagandha Powder - 1 Pound](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00GDINIZC?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support your nervous system during high-stress transitions, though no herb can substitute for structural change. The point isn't to chemically flatten your anger. The point is to resource yourself enough that you can think clearly about what you need to do next. ## What Happens When You Stop Performing Gratitude There's a script caregivers are supposed to follow. You're supposed to say you're grateful for the opportunity to serve. You're supposed to frame even the worst days as learning experiences. You're supposed to perform humility and thankfulness even when you're being paid poverty wages to do work that would break most people in a week. What happens when you stop? When you let yourself say, even just to yourself, "I am not grateful for this. I am angry about this"? At first, it might feel like falling. You've been holding yourself up with the story that this is noble, that suffering is part of the path, that your sacrifice means something. When you let that story go, there's a moment of vertigo. But then something else arrives. Clarity. Energy. The part of you that's been buried under the performance wakes up and says: I deserve better than this. Not in an entitled way. In a true way. You deserve to be paid fairly. You deserve to work in conditions that don't break your body. You deserve to care for others without having to choose between that and caring for yourself. This clarity is what anger was trying to give you all along. It's not trying to make you mean. It's trying to make you free. ## The Collective Rage and What It Could Build You're not angry alone. There are hundreds of thousands of caregivers carrying the same fury, the same exhaustion, the same knowledge that the system is rigged. That collective anger is a resource, not a problem. It's the raw material of organizing, of advocacy, of the kind of change that doesn't happen because people ask nicely. Unions for home care workers. Living wage campaigns. Legislation that treats caregiving as skilled labor. None of that happens without anger. Not the destructive kind, but the kind that says: we are done accepting crumbs, we are done being told our work doesn't count, we are worth more than this. When you feel that rage rising, you can channel it. You can join a caregiver support group, online or in person. You can connect with organizers in your area. You can share your story, not to perform trauma, but to add your voice to the chorus that's saying: this has to change. Some people find that [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps them sleep through the activation that comes with stepping into advocacy. Your body needs rest to sustain resistance. You can't fight on an empty tank. ## Anger as a Form of Self-Respect Here's what they don't tell you: the opposite of anger isn't peace. It's resignation. It's the slow death of believing you don't matter, that your needs are secondary, that your pain is the price of being good. Anger says: I matter. What I need matters. The conditions I work under matter. It's the last part of you that hasn't given up on the idea that you deserve dignity. When you feel it, you're not broken. You're awake. The fire toolkit we've developed includes practices for working with anger that don't require you to be alone with it. Sometimes anger needs to be witnessed, not just felt. Sometimes it needs to move through your body, through sound, through writing that no one will ever read, through the simple act of saying out loud: "I am so angry, and I have every right to be." You can also explore more articles in this journal that look at anger through different lenses, or visit the about page to understand the philosophy that shapes this work. If the Oracle voice lands, you might also find resources at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) that approach these themes from a more divination-centered angle. ## Three Questions Caregivers Actually Ask ### Is it wrong to feel angry at the people I care for? No. Feeling angry doesn't mean you don't care about them. It means you're human and you're overwhelmed. The anger is almost never really about the person in front of you. It's about the impossible situation you're both trapped in. What matters is that you don't act on that anger in ways that harm them, and that you find safe places to process it. A feeling isn't a moral failure. It's information. ### How do I know if I should quit or if I'm just burnt out? Burnout can recover with rest, boundary-setting, and support. If you take a week off and come back feeling like you can breathe again, that's burnout. If the thought of going back makes your chest tight and your stomach drop, if you've tried setting boundaries and they're ignored, if your health is deteriorating and nothing changes no matter what you do, that's a signal the job itself might be unsustainable. Trust what your body tells you over what guilt tells you. ### What if I can't afford to leave? This is the trap, and it's by design. Many caregivers stay in harmful situations because they can't afford the gap between jobs, because they need the health insurance, because they're supporting family. If you're in this position, start building an exit plan even if it takes months. Save what you can, even if it's $20 a week. Research other agencies or roles. Connect with other caregivers who've made the transition. You might not be able to leave tomorrow, but you can start moving toward the door. And sometimes, just knowing you're working toward an exit makes the present more bearable. --- The anger you carry isn't a burden you need to overcome. It's a compass pointing toward the life you're not living yet, the one where your labor is valued and your body doesn't pay the price for someone else's profit margin. The work of honoring that anger is slow. It doesn't happen in an article or a weekend or a single decision. It happens in the small moments when you choose to listen instead of suppress, when you let yourself say "this isn't okay" instead of "I should be grateful." It happens when you realize that anger and love aren't opposites, that you can rage against a system and still show up with tenderness for the people caught in it with you. The path forward isn't about becoming less angry. It's about becoming more awake to what the anger knows, and braver about what it's asking you to do. *Tool for this work: [Banyan Botanicals Gotu Kola Powder (3.5 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008X9JM68?tag=spankyspinola-20).* ## Keep reading - [the assessments hub](/assessments) - [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) - [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on the rage of underpaid caregivers, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
